title: Acquire Privileged User Status for the Uniface Router Monitor
breadcrumbs: Acquire Privileged User Status for the Uniface Router Monitor
text: Acquire Privileged User Status for the Uniface Router Monitor If you have privileged user status, you can use the Uniface Router Monitor to stop and start Uniface Servers, change Uniface Router settings, change and add Uniface server type (UST) settings, stop the Uniface Router, and reload the Uniface Router’s assignment file. Start the Router Monitor and connect it to the Uniface Router. For more information, see Connect the Uniface Router Monitor to the Uniface Router . On the Router Monitor’s main form, choose URouter >Authorize to display the Get privileged dialog box. Enter the user name and password for the account used by the Uniface Router. If the Uniface Router assignment file specifies the authorized user name with $PRIV_MON_USER , you must enter that user name and its password. If not, the default user name urouter is expected. A user account with that name must exist on the machine the Uniface Router is running on. If you do not have access to this user name and password, you can do one of the following: Edit the Uniface Router assignment file. Add or modify the $PRIV_MON_USER setting in the [SETTINGS] section, setting its value to your own user name. Then restart the Uniface Router. Define a user with name urouter . On Microsoft Windows, assign user urouter to the group Uniface Server Users . (This group is created by the Uniface Installer on Microsoft Windows and already has authority for Log on as a batch job .) Start the Uniface Router service as the current user. To do so: Stop the Uniface Router service. Go to the Services dialog, which is available in various ways depending on your Windows version. For example, In File Explorer, right-click Computer and choose Manage . Navigate to the Services item under Services and Applications . Click This account and enter the user and password, then grant this user the Log on as a batch job right. To start or stop servers from the Router Monitor, you also need to grant the current user the following rights: Act as part of the Operating System Create a token object Log on as a batch job Log on as a service Replace a process level token To grant these rights on Windows, open the Control Panel and choose Administrative Tools >Local Security Policy Choose Local Policies , User Rights Assignment , and add the rights. Restart the Uniface Router service. To have privileged user status by default: In the Uniface Router Monitor, choose File >Settings . Supply a default user name, but not a default password. The password you supply when you acquire privileged user status remains hidden and is not stored. To cancel privileged user status, choose URouter >Unauthorize . Related Links Uniface Router The Uniface Router is a multi-threaded process (urouter) responsible for interprocess communication in Uniface applications. It listens for requests from Uniface client applications and routes them to the appropriate Uniface Server, and it passes messages between various Uniface applications. Uniface Router Monitor The Uniface Router Monitor is a Uniface Windows application that you can use to fine-tune the performance of the Uniface Router while it is running. Authorizing Users on Microsoft Windows Platforms To authorize users to access the Uniface Server, you need to create a local user group on the Microsoft Windows system on which the Uniface Server will run. You can then add to the group the user accounts that are allowed to access the Server. $PRIV_MON_USER Specify the privileged user account under which the Uniface Router runs and which can be used to start and stop Uniface Routers and Servers using the Uniface Router Monitor or the UROUTMON component.
